governanceItems.label: Confirm you will provide open APIs with no data lock-in and full interoperability.
Confirm the FIS/TSYS retains full ownership of all data at all times, including during and after transition.
Confirm no proprietary data formats will be used — all data exportable in standard formats at any time.
Confirm full data extraction rights upon contract exit, at no additional cost to the client.
Confirm you can provide a side-by-side coexistence environment for a minimum 12-month dual-run period.
Confirm you will fund technical debt remediation where your technology introduction creates new technical debt.
Confirm you will provide AI governance documentation covering bias monitoring, model drift, and explainability for all AI tools deployed.
Confirm AI overlays can be integrated via API without requiring migration of core telephony or processing platforms.
Confirm the client retains ownership of telephony infrastructure and IVR design unless explicitly agreed otherwise.
Confirm a full coexistence architecture plan will be provided prior to go-live, covering the full transition period.
Confirm all tooling (including AI Overlays) will be pre approved by the FIS/TSYS Governance team, comprised of architecture, AI Governance and Security Operation, prior to implementation.
Confirm you will provide quantified, contractually referenceable projections of AHT reduction, call deflection, and cost impact from AI overlays.

migrationNotes:
Part 1 — Wave Composition: For each wave, list which client cohorts are included, explaining what criteria drove the grouping; for all clients, specify which client is mapped to each wave.  FIS has intentionally not prescribed a wave structure or grouping methodology. We expect vendors to analyze the client dataset provided and propose the wave design they believe is most operationally sound for an engagement of this complexity. There is no correct number of waves. Responses that demonstrate genuine analytical thinking about client risk, regulatory complexity, volume sequencing, and transition readiness will be evaluated more favorably than generic frameworks.

5% fallout from incumbent team post announcement of Rebadge activity. Sutherland will ensure backfill resources are hired and ready to support for continuity of operations.

Loss of key personnel and tribal knowledge once the rebadging annoucement has been made. Sutherland in partnership with FIS will manage the communication and change well to mitigate any fallout

Low risk. Sutherland will ensure the knowledge transfer is effective between the onsite & offshore teams

Impact in customer experience post migration to the new team. Sutherland will ensure the right resources are hired to deliver quality service

Loss of knowledge and continuity of cases while migrating between teams. Sutherland will ensure the documentation and case management is robust.

Low risk. Hire and train backfill resources in anticipation of attrition at onshore locations

evaluationBriefText:
SUTHERLAND
Vendor Evaluation Brief
FIS TMS Managed Services RFP | March 30, 2026 | Confidential
1. Vendor Files Identified
Sutherland_Response_to_FIS_TSYS.pdf (50 pages)
Sutherland_Appendix_B__TMS_RFP__Sutherland_Response_to_FIS.xlsx (10 tabs incl duplicate 9.0)
Sutherland_Response_to_Appendix_C_-_FIS_SOW.docx (SOW redlines)
Sutherland_Response_Guide.docx (Response guide)
2. Evaluation Tear Sheet
Total data points: 56
3. Completeness Summary
Total data points: 56
4. Executive Assessment Memo
SUTHERLAND
Bottom Line
Sutherland brings the strongest payments/issuer domain narrative in the field — trusted partner to 3 of top 5 global issuers, existing FIS CIM provider, 10,000+ rebadge track record — but delivers the highest TCV ($559.7M) and worst Year 1 ($-58.6M vs baseline) of any vendor. The 40% productivity target (18% OPEX + 22% Digital) and 95:5 offshore ratio are plausible but the certainty commitment is only 39.7%, the AI tool benefits have no dollar quantification, and the proposal leaves numerous key commercial fields as "TBD." The COLA-inclusive pricing for Y1–Y5 (2.5% baked in) is a structural advantage, but the overall economics trail the field by $70–135M in 5-year TCV.
Underlying Assumptions in their Proposal
Pricing: Total Cost of Ownership model preferred over hourly rates. COLA baked into rates for Y1–Y5 at 2.5% (US/UK/NL/DE) and 4% (PH/India/DR); COLA applies separately only from Y6. 7-year deal term assumed. Facility takeover of EMEA sites assumed including tech and non-tech assets. (Tab 6.0 C96–C108)
Offshoring: 95:5 offshore:onshore ratio across ALL geographies including UK/NL/DE. However, UK/NL/DE labor arbitrage is only 5% — the lowest EMEA arb in the field, suggesting Sutherland recognizes TUPE/regulatory limits even with high offshore ratios. NA voice goes to Philippines/DR, BO to India. (Tab 9.0 C8–C12)
AI/Digital: 19.3% AI/Digital overlay and 11.7% productivity gains for US & CA; same for EMEA. 5 AI tools proposed (IVA, Agent Assist, Agentic BO, Chatbot, Messaging) but NO dollar quantification for any tool. The 22% digital efficiency claim has no tool-level breakdown. (Tab 9.0 E8–F12; Tab 5.0 B51–B55)
Certainty: Only 39.7% — the lowest certainty commitment in the field. This means Sutherland is contractually backing less than 40% of claimed savings. (Tab 9.0 G8–G12)
Strengths & Differentiators
Existing FIS relationship + top-tier payments credentials: Only vendor (besides Genpact) with an active FIS engagement. Case studies include FIS (550+ FTE, 40% cost reduction), Fiserv (2,000+ FTE, 30% TCO), and Block (2,000+ FTE, 25% TCO). Serving 3 of top 5 global issuers covering 70% of global issuance. (Tab 3.0; Proposal p.5–9)
COLA-inclusive pricing provides cost predictability: Unlike Genpact (COLA-exclusive) or IBM (COLA=0%), Sutherland bakes 2.5% COLA into rates for Y1–Y5. This eliminates annual escalation negotiations and makes the TCV a true fixed-cost commitment. (Tab 6.0 C97–C108)
Industry-best attrition at 3.0–3.65%: By far the lowest voluntary attrition disclosed by any vendor (vs. industry averages of 20–40% for contact centers). This reduces ramp costs and preserves institutional knowledge. (Tab 2.0 D20)
Comprehensive facility takeover proposed: Sutherland assumes it will take over EMEA leased facilities including tech and non-tech assets, providing a cleaner operational separation than vendors who leave staff on FIS premises. (Tab 6.0 C106)
Key Risks & Concerns
Highest TCV in the field at $559.7M — $60–135M above competitors: Even with COLA-inclusive rates, Sutherland’s 5-year cost exceeds Cognizant ($499.9M), EXL ($477M), Genpact ($425M), and IBM ($460M). The Y1 cost of $202.8M includes significant rebadge/transition overhead. (Tab 6.0 C120–V120)
Year 1 is $-58.6M vs baseline — worst Y1 of any vendor: FIS would spend $58.6M MORE than its current cost in Year 1. This is driven by full rebadge of 95% of workforce across all sites simultaneously. The savings curve doesn’t turn positive until Y2 and doesn’t meet FIS targets until Y3. (Calculated)
Certainty commitment of only 39.7% creates significant risk: Less than 40% of the $190.9M claimed savings are contractually backed. At 39.7% certainty, the guaranteed savings are only ~$75.8M over 5 years. (Tab 9.0 G8)
Multiple key commercial fields left as "TBD": Minimum volume requirements, rebadge pre-conditions, and contract duration all marked TBD in Tab 6.5. This suggests the proposal is an indicative framework rather than a binding commercial offer. (Tab 6.0 D84–D88)
Key Questions to Pressure-Test in Workshop
Your TCV is $559.7M — $60–135M above competitors. What structural changes to your pricing model would you make to bring TCV below $500M? Which cost categories are negotiable?
Your certainty commitment is 39.7%. What would it take to raise this to 75%+? What contractual mechanism backs the committed portion?
You propose 95:5 offshore for UK/NL/DE but show only 5% labor arbitrage. If UK TUPE and German Works Council prevent reaching 95% offshore, what is the cost impact? Provide the sensitivity table.
Your AI tools have no dollar quantification. For the 22% digital efficiency claim, break down exactly which tools deliver which % reduction, with Year 1–Y5 benefits in dollars.
All investment conditions are "TBD." Is this a binding offer or an indicative framework? What conditions must be met before you convert to binding pricing?
Go-Forward Implications
Sutherland’s COLA-inclusive approach should become the evaluation standard: FIS should require all shortlisted vendors to present COLA-inclusive TCV to enable true like-for-like comparison. The difference between COLA-inclusive and COLA-exclusive pricing can be 15–25% over 7 years.
The facility takeover model (Sutherland assumes EMEA site leases) is an alternative FIS should evaluate against the "workforce only" approach used by Cognizant and others. Site takeover simplifies operational separation but transfers lease risk.
Sutherland’s low certainty commitment (39.7%) is a useful floor for negotiation: FIS should establish a minimum 75% certainty threshold as a qualification requirement for down-selection.

End to End Back Office and Front Office for CIM and Banking

Delivery Geos - US & India

Languages: English

Hyper-automation, Conversation AI, Agent Assist, Unified desktop, GenAI Knowledge mgmt.

55% Committed productivity 

40% cost reduction underwritten by Sutherland

90%+ re-badge

Challenges / Objectives:
Client faced high operational costs, poor customer/agent experience, limited digital adoption despite tech investments, and fragmented operations across GCC and multiple vendors.

The Sutherland Transformation:
Client selected Sutherland to provide L1/L2 CX and back-office support across Payments, Cards, Retail banking, Fraud alerts and Disputes. 
Sutherland executed a 5-year TCO-led transformation model with e2e process and digital transformation. Model had benefits front-loaded with risk-mitigated transformation roadmap to increase Y-On-Y savings
We designed a tailored transformation roadmap leveraging levers such as Lean Six Sigma led process optimization, hyper-automation, Conversational AI, Agent Assist, Unified Desktop, and GenAI knowledge management.
We committed to a custom commercial and target operating model with 40% cost reduction underwritten by Sutherland, 90%+ re-badge for ops consolidation/continuity, and 55% productivity gains.

FISERV

Card Services, Clover Support, Payment Services, Credit Union Solutions, Biller Services, Disputes & Fraud, Data security , Merchant POS support, Technical support and compliance

Delivery Geos: India, LATAM, EMEA

Languages: English, Spanish, German & Polish

Transformation Led operations backed by Design Labs, Sutherland Digital tools and partnerships

30% TCO reduction through innovative commercial models, digital transformation

100% Compliance on PII data masking

100% NPS rating over last 7 years

Challenges / Objectives:
A leading global fintech provider faced spiraling costs, fragmented siloed processes, high fraud losses, poor customer satisfaction, and data security risks from thousands of client systems, needing rapid tech stack optimization and intelligent automation

The Sutherland Transformation:
Full process redesign and optimization while integrating clients siloed systems for best risk mitigation and compliance.
A right-shoring strategy, enabling a phased transition to Sutherland's Banking and Financial Services Centers of Excellence around the globe. 
Sutherland Sentinel AI deployed to give real-time employee authentication with data masking of PII data for all client systems.
Sutherland Connect, Speech Analytics, and intelligent automation via Sutherland Robility and Sutherland Extract, transforming their operating model.

Guaranteed 20% ramp with a 30-day notice period

Committed 15-20% internal seeding for new LOB launches

Client needed a strategic global partner for growth/consolidation, domain expertise across front-middle-back office functions with AI/tech, rapid scaling, and outcome-based commercials to hit Rule of 40 while cutting costs and boosting quality/service. Client expected high scalability and agility to rapidly adopt to their dynamic business needs.

Nimble global right-shoring model implemented across 2 sites in Philippines and India in a brick & mortar operating model
Designed CoE led delivery model. CoEs set up for CX, Back Office and Risk Operations delivery across 2 countries with embedded domain SMEs

snippets.questionText: For each requirement listed in Column C, provide your response in Column D. Responses should be specific to this engagement and the regulatory environments in scope. Where certifications or formal frameworks apply, state them.
snippets.text:
Specific Requirement

Vendor Response — How Your Solution Meets This Requirement

Disclose all sub-contractors by name, location, and scope. Confirm SLA flow-down and FIS audit rights over all 4th parties.

For this engagement, we may plan to use multiple sub-contractors. For e.g., we plan to use Uniphore as a specialized sub contractor to provide the unified desktop and real time agent assist solution that underpins our digital transformation and CX optimization objectives. Uniphore’s platform will be used to deliver unified agent workflows and real time guidance from approved locations, while all core service delivery, governance, and client facing roles remain with Sutherland as the prime contractor. We are utilizing Uniphore as a sub-contractor to provide AI services to FIS already.
There maybe a need to evaluate possible use of additional sub-contractors to deliver services in EMEA region. We also constantly evaluate best in industry AI solutions to bring the best of global AI tools and technology to deliver the transformation solutions we are proposing as part of our response. Should any additional sub contractors be required as the engagement evolves, Sutherland will notify the client in advance, provide full details of scope, location, and controls, and obtain the client’s prior written approval before onboarding.

2.	SLA flow down mechanism
All SLAs, security, confidentiality, data protection, and regulatory obligations agreed with the client are contractually flowed down to our sub-contractors through back to back agreements that mirror the requirements in the prime contract. We operate a single integrated SLA and governance framework in which our sub-contractors performance is monitored alongside Sutherland’s operations via common dashboards and review forums, with defined remediation actions and the right to invoke service credits or adjust or terminate the sub contract arrangement in the event of persistent under performance. In summary, overall performance of this engagement (including anything sent to sub-contractor) will be the responsibility of Sutherland.

3.	Client audit rights over sub contractors
The client will retain full audit and inspection rights, as defined in the master services agreement, over material sub contractors used in delivering TMS services, including our sub-contractors. These rights (such as access to relevant records, processes, and control evidence, subject to reasonable notice and confidentiality) are embedded in our contract with our partner sub-contractors to ensure that the client’s and regulators’ audit and oversight requirements can be met seamlessly across the delivery chain.

Describe your internal governance model for monitoring sub-contractor performance and compliance.

Sutherland uses a standardized third‑party risk and vendor governance framework to oversee all sub‑contractors supporting our services. Sub‑contractors are risk‑tiered, with clear ownership, SLAs/KPIs aligned to client commitments, and regular performance reviews and scorecards. Compliance is monitored through periodic risk assessments, security and data‑protection checks, incident reporting and remediation, and participation in internal and client/regulatory audits, with contractual remedies available where performance or compliance falls below agreed thresholds.

Confirm cardholder data will not leave approved jurisdictions without explicit client consent. Describe data residency controls.

To confirm, cardholder data (CHD) is strictly confined to approved jurisdictions through a combination of geographic pinning and rigorous technical egress filters, ensuring no data crosses borders without explicit client consent. This is enforced by hosting sensitive workloads in regional "silos"—physically isolated data centers—where infrastructure-as-code policies block the provisioning of storage in unauthorized territories. To prevent accidental or malicious transfers, Data Loss Prevention (DLP) tools and geofencing are deployed to monitor and intercept outbound traffic containing Primary Account Numbers (PANs). These residency controls are validated through continuous automated data discovery and annual audits to maintain compliance with both regional legal mandates and PCI DSS standards
